Coconut Reproduction Report

  1. what you did and how Coconut works in your own words (the continuous-thought feedback + curriculum)

I followed the original Coconut paper’s description of the architecture heavily. The model works by slowly converting CoT-learned representation to latent-capable one using the curriculum learning. Since the objective is to iterate over the latent space, which the model wasn’t trained to do, the curriculum learning approach slowly converts each step to latent thinking over multiple epochs, before proceeding to the next.

The training first takes a pretrained LLM and supervised finetunes it using a chain-of-thought dataset, such as GSM8K. Then, a <bot> and <eot> token is inserted before the first step, and the model trained for a given number of epochs (3 in my case). Afterwards, each natural language step is converted to k latent tokens placed between the <bot> and <eot> tokens starting from the the first step, and the model trained for a set number of epochs for each “stage” before repeating with the next step. This continues until all language steps are converted to latent steps.

The training uses a cross-entropy loss between the natural langauge tokens produced after the <eot> token and the corresponding ground truth tokens in the training set. During evaluation, the question part of the dataset is given, with a pre-set number of latent steps between the <bot> and the <eot> token. The model starts autoregressively generating the hidden states to be used in latent reasoning starting at the <bot> token, and repeats for the predefined number of latent steps. The final answer is extracted by splitting the generated natural language text via “#”, a format that was used consistently during CoT finetuning and the curriculum learning.

  1. the problems / issues you hit and how you solved them (heavily weighted — the sequential latent forward pass, gradient flow through fed-back hidden states, tokenizer/marker handling, loss masking, curriculum scheduling, etc.);

During the reconstruction of the original Coconut training regimen, I did encounter a few design choices that impacted the training result. The most difficult issue I came across was the batch processing of latent reasoning for efficient training. Since the latent reasoning process is sequential by nature, the training cannot simply predict the next token by using a single sequence as input and move to the next batch. However, the prompts are of varying length; some prompts are longer than others, so the latent steps are computed at different time steps, making batching difficult. This was solved by left-padding all prompts until the <bot> tokens aligned to the same time step.

Another issue was the lack of VRAM. The batched training of a LLM, despite the smaller parameter size of 0.6 billion, still required heavy compute to utilize an acceptable batch size of 32. To mitigate this, bfloat16 was used for all computation, and a 8-bit optimizer was used to reduce optimizer state VRAM consumption. In accordance with this, the embedding layer on the model was changed to StableEmbedding from bitsandbytes.

A third, slightly minor issue was that gradient accumulation logic did not originally function as intended. The batch size was frequently reduced due to out-of-memory errors, but adjusting the accumulation variable resulted in a different evaluation accuracy. The cause for this was due to erroneous loss calculation. The mean cross entropy loss was being averaged over gradient_accumulation_steps. After changing it so that the sum cross entropy loss was averaged over the number of effective non-masked tokens, the accumulation worked a lot better.

The last major issue I faced was the difficulty of retaining mathematical capability during the latent transition. Despite trying multiple different configurations, the GSM8k test accuracy degrades as training progresses. In fact, catastrophic collapse was encountered quite a few times. I wasn’t able to find a solution to this, but I did find that lower learning rates reduced the degradation.

  1. the overfit-one-example evidence: the overfitting code, the generated text vs. the ground-truth text, and the loss curve;

For the overfit-one example, the code can be run using python main.py configs/gsm_coconut_single.yaml. Initially, I planned to attempt training with multiple different k configurations and training curriculum with different epochs per stage, but surprisingly the problems overfit easily on the first epoch of k=1. 10 prompts to overfit were chosen from the pool of evaluation prompts that were incorrectly solved by the CoT-finetuned model. All of them achieved near-zero loss (0.0000) and correct accuracy after overfitting. The questions used were {3, 6, 8, 9, 11, 16, 17, 18, 21, 24}.

  1. if reached: GSM8K accuracy (and any baseline comparison).
